Prompt:
[ROLE]
You are a psychiatrist conducting a diagnostic assessment. Read the patient case and follow the instructions to provide a diagnosis. Note: Not all patients will have a diagnosis.

[PATIENT CASE]
Referral:  Mr. WM is a 45-year-old truck driver who was referred by his primary care physician.  Mr. WM mentioned that he was having difficulty on the job since having been in a road crash with his vehicle about one year ago. He spent a week in the hospital recovering from his injuries from the crash, and he stated that he felt lucky to be alive afterwards.
Presenting Symptoms:  Since the crash, Mr. WM feels constantly nervous and jumps when he hears loud noises, particularly when walking along the roadway.  When he hears cars braking at high speeds, he feels his arms go forward and his foot involuntarily pushing on an imaginary brake.  Preceding a road trip, his sleep is severely disrupted, due to nightmares about the crash. He paces around the house and feels chest pains along his rib cage where he was pinned down by the steering wheel. He has avoided doing any driving during his off time at home and does not interact with his driving buddies because he cannot tolerate hearing stories of “near misses” on the road.  When it is time to get into his truck, he circles around the vehicle many times before he gets up the courage to go in.  A couple of times this past year, he could not get in and called in sick, losing the assignment and pay.
Additional Background Information:  Mr. WM has had a generally good relationship with his wife of 20 years, although she is losing patience with his behavior, which has significantly reduced their income. His relationships with his children are somewhat distant due to his constant travel.  A neurological exam and CAT scan reveal nothing abnormal.   His ribs and other injuries have healed well, and should not be causing any further pain.

[INSTRUCTIONS]
Please carefully read the patient case and follow these steps:
1. Review the symptoms, their severity, their duration, whether they have occurred before (recurrence), and impact on functioning
2. Review the list below of potential diagnoses from ICD11, focusing on understanding the key differences between each diagnosis
3. Select the 3 most likely diagnoses from the list below in descending order, keeping in mind that the person may not have any diagnosis
3. Provide a brief explanation (1 sentences) for why these diagnoses best fit the case, citing specific symptoms and criteria

Potential stress-related diagnoses (ICD11):
- Post-Traumatic Stress Disorder (PTSD)
- Complex Post-Traumatic Stress Disorder (CPTSD)
- Prolonged Grief Disorder
- Adjustment Disorder
- Other Disorder Specifically Associated with Stress
- Acute Stress Reaction
- Other Diagnosis
- No Diagnosis

[FORMAT]
Most Likely Diagnosis: [diagnosis]
Reasoning: [2-3 sentences]

Second Most Likely: [diagnosis]
Reasoning: [1-2 sentences]

Third Most Likely: [diagnosis]
Reasoning: [1-2 sentences]

**WARNING:** Any other output format is incorrect.
Model_Output:
Most Likely Diagnosis: Post-Traumatic Stress Disorder (PTSD)
Reasoning: Mr. WM's symptoms, such as nightmares about the crash, avoidance of driving, and physiological responses to loud noises or high-speed braking, are consistent with the diagnostic criteria for PTSD. His symptoms have been present for over a year, indicating a chronic condition, and have significantly impacted his daily functioning, including his work and relationships.

Second Most Likely: Adjustment Disorder
Reasoning: While Mr. WM's symptoms are more severe and chronic than those typically seen in adjustment disorder, his symptoms could be considered adjustment disorder if they are not severe enough to meet the full criteria for PTSD. However, his avoidance of driving and significant impact on his daily functioning make PTSD a more likely diagnosis.

Third Most Likely: Acute Stress Reaction
Reasoning: Mr. WM's symptoms have been present for over a year, which is longer than the typical duration for an acute stress reaction. However, his symptoms could be considered acute stress reaction if they are not severe enough to meet the full criteria for PTSD or adjustment disorder.

He has nightmares at least once a week in which he dreams he is trapped and feels frightened and panicky. He wakes up from these dreams covered in sweat. When he enters a closed space like an elevator, he can feel pressure on his chest just like what happened in the accident. Because of his problems, he has been less active in his work and the family income has all but ceased, and he has become much less sociable.
Additional Background Information: DB has no prior history of mental disorder and is proud of never having been ill in his life. He is known in his community as a very tough character who fears nobody. He has a wife and three children but nobody was at home when the ceiling collapsed. She has been in the country for 6 years, and is a refugee from a country with ongoing civil upheaval. Her whole family was arrested and persecuted in their home country before she and her brothers were able to immigrate to their current place.
Presenting Symptoms:  She describes herself as totally exhausted. She claims not being able to sleep well since leaving her home country with only 2-3 hours sleep per night. She indicates that she suffers from reliving the several arrests (of herself, her father, and her brothers) and her own prison experiences. If she talks about these experiences, she gets agitated and cries heavily. Upon being asked about nightmares, she affirms having them multiple times a week but does not want to go into detail about her experiences, which she finds too difficult to discuss. She constantly feels irritated, finds no inner peace and is afraid that she continues to be followed. For this reason, she prefers not to be around strangers, as she feels they could be dangerous. Thus, she stays at home most days and has stopped attending classes at the university altogether. As a result, she is likely to fail all her classes and be expelled from the university. She reports that she feels no pleasure anymore. Instead, she says that she feels numb most of the time. She fears never being happy again and is fearful she will never succeed at anything again. She thinks she was a bad daughter because she could not save her beloved father who disappeared in prison and feels like a failure and very guilty at the thought of her father’s suffering. The relationship with her husband also feels cold and without any emotion; consequently, he spends less time at home and more time with his friends from the old country. Her husband tries to be supportive of her but she does not accept his gestures or even see that he is making an effort. She says she is likely to yell at him over “the littlest thing” with very minimal provocation, further creating distance between them. She says that her husband is considering a divorce.
Additional Background Information:  In her home country, she was imprisoned several times beginning at age 24, and spent a total of 18 months in prison. She talks in general terms about her experience of sexual abuse by the prison guards but does not want to give details. She says “It happened almost every day.” Her father was arrested shortly before her and has been missing ever since. She met her husband in her current country; he originally comes from a neighboring country. Her childhood and adolescence were well protected. She described herself as ambitious and capable. In the immediate aftermath he seemed to be coping well. They were able to bury their mother and hold a funeral. After repairs, his home is now safe. The daughters had hoped that he would have returned to work by now. However, CC says he cannot think about doing this at the moment, as it is unbearable to go to work without his wife. He sits at home most of the day, alone, sometimes listening to the radio. The daughters are also concerned by his loss of weight and the fact that sometimes they hear him talking with his dead wife as if she were still there. 
Presenting Symptoms: In the interview, CC is polite but withdrawn. He is neatly dressed in clean shirt and trousers and sits calmly, speaking very softly, but at normal speed. He answers all questions asked. He agrees that he has not been himself since his wife died. He asks the doctor if any man could be ‘normal’ in such a situation. He thinks about his wife all the time and spends a lot of time remembering their life together. These are actually happy memories, and this is why he prefers not to be interrupted or distracted by others when he is thinking about her. He has no unpleasant intrusive memories of the earthquake or nightmares. He knows in some way this is not true, but when it happens, it feels real, and he thinks she is visiting and looking after him, consistent with his religious beliefs. He likes to talk to her and does not see why he should not do so as it comforts him. 
Additional Background Information: CC was married to his wife for 30 years and they were rarely apart. The shop was their joint creation and occupation. He no longer spends time with his usual group of friends, and seems quite isolated. DD has told the teacher he has thought of abandoning school and joining his brother’s military unit, but his father has forbidden it. DD sees JD as a hero and wishes he had joined up with him. DD watches a home video of the funeral at least once a week and has created a small shrine/memorial to JD in his bedroom. He realises he is doing badly at school and recognises he might fail some important exams, but it does not seem very important any more. He cannot discuss his feelings with his old friends as he recognises they are bored with him by now, so he keeps to himself. He is not suicidal. His sleep and appetite are normal, although he feels tired most days. Background additional information DDs parents explain that DD and his brother were very close from DD’s birth. His parents also idolized their eldest son. Both wept during the interview. They wanted advice as to how to help their younger son. They thought the funeral video and shrine had gone on too long, but did not want to challenge DD and upset him further.
